text_summary: CIBMTR registry analysis of 1297 patients receiving axicabtagene ciloleucel for relapsed/refractory LBCL in commercial setting (2018-2022). Real-world ORR was 73% with 54% CR, compared to 83% ORR and 58% CR in the ZUMA-1 pivotal trial. Median OS was 19.8 months with a 2-year OS rate of 47%. Patients were older (median 63 years) and had more comorbidities than trial populations, suggesting the efficacy gap is driven by broader patient selection.

CIBMTR registry outcomes for tisagenlecleucel in 506 adult DLBCL patients treated commercially. ORR was 62% with 40% CR, compared to 52% ORR and 40% CR in JULIET. Median PFS was 5.9 months. Grade 3+ CRS occurred in 8% and grade 3+ neurotoxicity in 6%, lower than pivotal trial rates. The lower severe toxicity rate in real-world likely reflects improved CRS management and earlier tocilizumab use.

CIBMTR analysis of 412 patients receiving lisocabtagene maraleucel for relapsed/refractory LBCL. Real-world ORR was 69% with 52% CR, closely matching the TRANSCEND NHL 001 trial results of 73% ORR and 53% CR. Grade 3+ CRS was 3% and grade 3+ ICANS was 8%. The close alignment with pivotal data may reflect the TRANSCEND trial's broader eligibility criteria, which better represented real-world patients.

CIBMTR registry data on 987 patients receiving idecabtagene vicleucel for relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma after 4+ prior lines. Real-world ORR was 71% with 33% CR/sCR, compared to 73% ORR and 33% CR in KarMMa. Median PFS was 8.8 months. Grade 3+ CRS occurred in 5% and grade 3+ neurotoxicity in 4%. Results suggest commercial ide-cel outcomes are concordant with the pivotal trial in multiple myeloma.

Multi-center retrospective study comparing CAR-T outcomes at 12 high-volume academic centers (>50 infusions/year) vs 24 community-based certified centers. Academic centers had higher CR rates (57% vs 44%, p=0.003) and lower 100-day NRM (3.1% vs 5.8%, p=0.02). ICU transfer rates were 28% at academic vs 39% at community sites. The volume-outcome relationship suggests a minimum of 20-25 infusions per year for optimal outcomes.

Analysis of 193 patients treated at community oncology centers newly certified for CAR-T administration. Early experience (first 10 patients) showed higher grade 3+ CRS (18%) vs later experience (8%, p=0.04). 30-day mortality decreased from 6.2% to 2.1% as institutional experience grew. Time from leukapheresis to infusion was longer at community sites (median 35 vs 28 days at academic centers), reflecting coordination challenges with manufacturing logistics.

Single high-volume academic center experience with 387 consecutive CAR-T infusions across all products (2018-2023). Center achieved 76% ORR, 56% CR for LBCL indications. Standardized CRS management protocol with early tocilizumab (within 12 hours of grade 2) reduced ICU transfers from 35% to 18% over the study period. Dedicated CAR-T coordinators reduced median time from referral to infusion from 52 to 38 days.

Retrospective analysis of 348 patients aged 65 years or older receiving commercial CAR-T for LBCL across 15 US centers. ORR was 68% with 48% CR, not significantly different from younger patients (72% ORR, 53% CR, p=0.14). However, grade 3+ CRS was higher in elderly (15% vs 9%, p=0.01) and ICU admission was 38% vs 25%. Non-relapse mortality at 1 year was 8.2% in elderly vs 4.1% in younger patients. Baseline comorbidity index was a stronger predictor than age alone.

Analysis of 214 multiple myeloma patients aged 65-80 receiving idecabtagene vicleucel or ciltacabtagene autoleucel. ORR was 69% with 30% sCR. Patients aged 70-80 had higher grade 3+ CRS (12% vs 6%) and prolonged cytopenias lasting >60 days (41% vs 24%) compared to 65-69 year olds. Median PFS was 9.1 months for the full cohort. Baseline frailty assessment correlated with toxicity risk better than chronological age.

CIBMTR analysis of 127 B-ALL patients aged 40-75 receiving tisagenlecleucel. Patients ≥55 (n=48) had similar CR rates to younger adults (81% vs 85%) but higher 1-year NRM (14% vs 6%, p=0.03). Prolonged B-cell aplasia led to more infectious complications in older patients. Median OS was 18.2 months for elderly vs 25.6 months for younger adults, partly driven by reduced salvage options after CAR-T failure in the older cohort.

Retrospective analysis of 523 LBCL patients comparing outcomes with bridging therapy (n=341) vs no bridging (n=182) prior to axicabtagene ciloleucel. Bridging was associated with lower CR rate (48% vs 61%, p=0.004) and shorter median PFS (5.2 vs 11.8 months). However, patients receiving bridging had higher disease burden at referral. After propensity score matching, bridging itself was not an independent predictor of PFS, suggesting disease biology drives outcomes rather than bridging per se.

Analysis of bridging therapy regimens in 289 patients awaiting CAR-T. Chemotherapy bridging (R-GemOx, polatuzumab-BR) was used in 61%, radiation in 22%, and steroids-only in 17%. Radiation bridging was associated with best outcomes (ORR 74%) likely due to selection of localized disease. Chemotherapy bridging with polatuzumab-BR yielded 52% ORR post-CAR-T. Progressive disease during bridging (31% of patients) was the strongest negative predictor of CAR-T response (ORR 38% vs 71%).

Single-center study of 94 LBCL patients who received no bridging therapy before CAR-T, representing 28% of their total referrals. These patients had lower tumor burden (median SPD 18 vs 42 cm2) and better ECOG performance status. ORR was 79% with 62% CR. Median PFS was 14.7 months. The no-bridging group had lower grade 3+ CRS (6% vs 14%) and shorter hospital stays (median 10 vs 16 days), consistent with lower baseline inflammatory burden.

Five-year follow-up of 267 patients treated with axicabtagene ciloleucel for LBCL in the commercial setting (2018-2019 infusions). Five-year OS was 40.2% and 5-year PFS was 31.8%. Among patients in CR at 12 months (n=128), the 5-year PFS was 72%, indicating durable remission in early responders. Late relapses beyond 24 months were rare (4.7%). Late-onset toxicities included prolonged hypogammaglobulinemia (63%) requiring IVIG and persistent cytopenias at 1 year (12%).

Three-year outcomes from 184 patients treated with tisagenlecleucel for B-ALL (pediatric and young adult). Three-year OS was 56% and event-free survival was 44%. Among patients achieving MRD-negative CR (76%), 3-year EFS was 58%. Loss of B-cell aplasia (BCA) preceded relapse in 72% of cases. Late neurotoxicity was not observed. Cumulative incidence of subsequent allogeneic SCT was 18% at 3 years, mostly for patients with early loss of BCA.

Three-year real-world follow-up of 309 multiple myeloma patients receiving ciltacabtagene autoleucel. Median PFS was 21.8 months with 3-year PFS of 34%. Three-year OS was 58%. Among patients achieving sCR (41%), 3-year PFS was 62%. Late movement and neurocognitive toxicity (MNT) occurred in 5.2%, lower than initial clinical trial reports (7%), possibly due to improved recognition and early intervention. MRD negativity at 10^-5 was the strongest predictor of durable response.

Resource utilization analysis of 618 commercial CAR-T infusions across 8 centers. ICU admission rate was 29%, with median ICU stay of 3 days (IQR 2-6). ICU admission was driven by CRS (68%), ICANS (21%), or both (11%). Patients receiving axicabtagene ciloleucel had higher ICU rates (35%) vs lisocabtagene maraleucel (18%). Early tocilizumab protocols reduced ICU transfers by 38%. Median total hospital length of stay was 16 days (IQR 11-24) for inpatient CAR-T administration.

Insurance claims analysis of 30-day and 90-day readmission rates following CAR-T therapy. Among 1842 patients, 30-day readmission was 34% and 90-day readmission was 48%. Most common readmission causes: infection (31%), cytopenias/febrile neutropenia (24%), delayed neurotoxicity (12%), and disease progression (9%). Median cost of index hospitalization was $468,000 and 90-day total cost including readmissions was $612,000. Outpatient CAR-T administration (7% of cohort) had 22% lower 30-day readmission.

EHR-linked claims analysis of total cost of care for CAR-T therapy in 743 LBCL patients over 1 year. Median 1-year total cost was $587,000 including product acquisition ($373,500), hospitalization ($124,000), and outpatient follow-up ($89,500). Patients with grade 3+ CRS/ICANS had $218,000 higher costs. Patients in CR at 6 months had lower 6-12 month costs ($12,400) vs non-responders ($87,600). Cost-effectiveness improved when used in second-line vs third-line due to better outcomes and shorter prior treatment costs.

SEER-Medicare linked analysis of 4287 LBCL patients eligible for CAR-T (2018-2023). Only 11.3% received CAR-T. Black patients were 42% less likely to receive CAR-T than White patients (OR 0.58, 95% CI 0.44-0.76) after adjusting for age, comorbidities, and insurance. Hispanic patients had 35% lower access (OR 0.65). Geographic distance to certified center >50 miles reduced access by 51%. Rural patients had the lowest CAR-T utilization (5.2%) compared to urban (13.8%).

Multi-center analysis of CAR-T outcomes by race/ethnicity in 672 patients who received commercial CAR-T for LBCL. ORR was similar across groups: White 72%, Black 68%, Hispanic 70% (p=0.51). However, Black patients had higher NRM at 1 year (9.1% vs 4.8%, p=0.04) and lower 2-year OS (41% vs 52%, p=0.03). Differences were partly explained by higher comorbidity burden, longer time from diagnosis to CAR-T (median 18.2 vs 13.4 months), and more advanced disease at referral in Black patients.

Retrospective analysis of 156 patients who received CAR-T after prior allogeneic stem cell transplant. ORR was 64% with 42% CR, numerically lower than non-allo patients (73% ORR). Graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) flare occurred in 9% of patients, all with prior chronic GVHD history. Grade 3+ CRS rates were similar to non-allo patients. Median PFS was 5.4 months. Donor-derived CAR-T cells (from the allograft) were detected in 78% of products, raising questions about the functional impact of donor vs recipient T cells.

CIBMTR analysis of 89 pediatric/young adult B-ALL patients receiving tisagenlecleucel after prior allogeneic SCT. CR rate was 82%, comparable to non-allo patients (88%). GVHD reactivation occurred in 6.7%, all manageable with steroids. One-year EFS was 42% and OS was 61%. Importantly, prior allo-SCT was not a negative prognostic factor for CAR-T response in B-ALL. Manufacturing success rate was 97%, with products containing predominantly donor-derived T cells.

Multi-center retrospective of 83 LBCL patients with active or prior CNS involvement who received commercial CAR-T. CNS response rate was 56% with 38% CNS CR. Grade 3+ ICANS was higher than non-CNS cohorts (28% vs 12%), with 4 patients requiring prolonged ICU care. No CAR-T-related deaths in the CNS group. Median PFS was 4.2 months and median OS was 9.7 months. Active CNS disease at infusion (n=47) had worse outcomes than prior CNS disease in remission (n=36), with PFS of 2.8 vs 7.1 months.

Analysis of 52 B-ALL patients with CNS-2 or CNS-3 status who received tisagenlecleucel. CNS clearance was achieved in 71% of patients. Grade 3+ ICANS occurred in 23% but was reversible in all cases. Median EFS was 8.4 months. Patients with isolated CNS relapse (n=14) had better outcomes (EFS 14.2 months) than combined medullary and CNS disease (n=38, EFS 6.1 months). CAR-T cell detection in CSF correlated with CNS response, supporting active trafficking across the blood-brain barrier.

Multi-center experience with second CAR-T infusion in 74 LBCL patients who relapsed after initial CAR-T. The same product was used in 41 patients and a different product in 33. ORR to second infusion was 34% (same product 24%, different product 47%, p=0.03). Median duration of response was 4.1 months. Grade 3+ CRS was 8% on retreatment. CD19 expression was confirmed positive in 81% of retreated patients. Loss of CD19 was the primary mechanism of resistance in non-responders to both first and second infusion.

Analysis of 41 B-ALL patients who received a second CAR-T infusion (tisagenlecleucel re-infusion n=23, humanized CD19 CAR n=18) after relapse with CD19-positive disease. CR rate was 46% overall (39% same product, 56% different construct). Median EFS from second infusion was 5.8 months. Anti-CAR antibodies (HAMA) were detected in 35% of re-infused patients and correlated with poor expansion and lower response rate. Lymphodepletion with fludarabine/cyclophosphamide before second infusion improved outcomes.

Retrospective analysis of 438 LBCL patients examining impact of time from last relapse to CAR-T infusion. Patients infused within 60 days of progression (n=186) had ORR 78% vs 64% for those infused >90 days after progression (n=252, p=0.001). Median OS was 28.4 months vs 15.1 months respectively. Shorter time to infusion correlated with lower tumor burden (median SPD 22 vs 48 cm2) and better performance status. Vein-to-vein time >45 days was associated with significantly worse outcomes.

Meta-analysis of real-world studies comparing second-line vs third-line or later CAR-T in LBCL, incorporating data from 2847 patients across 14 studies post-ZUMA-7/TRANSFORM/BELINDA era. Second-line CAR-T (n=1123) showed ORR 79% with 65% CR and 2-year PFS of 51%, significantly superior to third-line (n=1724) with ORR 68%, 49% CR, and 2-year PFS 33% (p<0.001). Grade 3+ CRS was similar (10% vs 12%). Earlier CAR-T use was associated with 37% reduction in overall mortality (HR 0.63, 95% CI 0.54-0.74).

DESCAR-T French national registry analysis of 892 patients receiving commercial CAR-T for LBCL (2019-2023). ORR was 66% with 47% CR. Median PFS was 6.1 months and median OS was 16.8 months. Grade 3+ CRS occurred in 7% and grade 3+ ICANS in 10%. Outcomes were similar to US CIBMTR data despite different healthcare system structures. French centers had slightly longer vein-to-vein times (median 38 days) due to centralized manufacturing logistics. EBMT cross-validation confirmed concordant results across European registries.

CIBMTR registry analysis of 305 patients aged 65 years and older receiving axicabtagene ciloleucel for relapsed/refractory LBCL in the commercial setting (2018-2023). ORR was 65% with 44% CR, compared to 73% ORR and 54% CR in the overall CIBMTR axi-cel population. Median PFS was 5.8 months and median OS was 14.7 months. Grade 3+ CRS was 16% and grade 3+ ICANS was 22%, both higher than the all-age cohort (12% and 15% respectively). Non-relapse mortality at 1 year was 10.2% vs 5.1% in younger patients (p=0.002). Despite attenuated outcomes, 38% of elderly patients achieving CR at 6 months remained in durable remission at 2 years, confirming meaningful benefit. Comorbidity burden (HCT-CI score) was more predictive of toxicity than chronological age alone.

Nationwide insurance claims analysis of geographic disparities in CAR-T therapy access across 2.1 million commercially insured and Medicare Advantage patients with LBCL, B-ALL, or multiple myeloma diagnosed between 2018-2024. Only 8.7% of eligible patients were referred to CAR-T certified centers. Referral rates varied 5-fold by region: Northeast 14.2%, West 10.8%, South 6.3%, Midwest 7.1%. Patients residing >60 miles from a REMS-certified center had 58% lower odds of receiving CAR-T (OR 0.42, 95% CI 0.36-0.49). Rural patients had the lowest utilization (3.8% vs 11.4% urban). Racial disparities persisted after geographic adjustment: Black patients had 38% lower referral rates than White patients in the same metropolitan areas. Satellite apheresis collection programs increased referral rates by 2.1-fold in underserved regions.

Retrospective multi-center analysis comparing lisocabtagene maraleucel outcomes at 18 community-based certified centers (n=198) vs 14 high-volume academic centers (n=214) for relapsed/refractory LBCL. Community ORR was 66% with 48% CR, compared to academic ORR of 71% with 54% CR (p=0.28 for ORR). Median PFS was not significantly different: 7.2 months community vs 8.8 months academic (HR 1.18, p=0.19). Grade 3+ CRS rates were comparable (3% vs 2%), consistent with liso-cel's favorable toxicity profile across settings. Grade 3+ ICANS was 9% community vs 7% academic (p=0.42). Time from referral to infusion was longer at community sites (median 42 vs 33 days, p=0.003). The comparable outcomes suggest liso-cel's defined CD4:CD8 composition and lower toxicity profile facilitate safe administration outside high-volume academic centers.

CIBMTR registry 5-year follow-up of 410 pediatric and young adult (age 3-25) B-ALL patients treated with tisagenlecleucel in the commercial setting (2018-2020 infusions). Five-year OS was 52% and 5-year event-free survival (EFS) was 42%. Among the 337 patients (82%) achieving MRD-negative CR at day 28, 5-year EFS was 48%. Patients with detectable CAR-T cells at 12 months (44% of evaluable patients) had 5-year EFS of 67% vs 28% for those with undetectable CARs (p<0.001). Late relapses beyond 24 months occurred in 8.3%, predominantly CD19-negative (62%) or CD19-dim (24%) variants. Cumulative incidence of subsequent allogeneic SCT was 22% at 5 years, predominantly in patients with early B-cell recovery. Long-term toxicities included persistent hypogammaglobulinemia (71%) and growth velocity reduction in prepubertal patients (14%).

Multi-center retrospective analysis of 48 patients with primary or secondary CNS lymphoma treated with axicabtagene ciloleucel off-label at 9 academic centers (2019-2024). Primary CNS lymphoma (PCNSL, n=22) achieved ORR 68% with 45% CR, while secondary CNS lymphoma (SCNSL, n=26) achieved ORR 54% with 35% CR. Median PFS was 7.8 months for PCNSL and 4.1 months for SCNSL. Grade 3+ ICANS occurred in 33% (higher than systemic LBCL), with 3 patients requiring prolonged ICU care for cerebral edema. No CAR-T-related deaths occurred. CSF analysis demonstrated detectable CAR-T cells in 87% of patients by day 14, confirming CNS trafficking. Patients who received intrathecal chemotherapy bridging (n=18) had numerically higher CR rates (56% vs 33%) but the comparison was not powered. Median OS was 14.2 months for the full cohort.

Retrospective analysis of 280 LBCL patients examining the impact of bridging therapy response on subsequent CAR-T outcomes across 11 US academic centers. Patients were categorized by bridging response: complete/partial response (n=78, 28%), stable disease (n=89, 32%), and progressive disease (n=113, 40%). Post-CAR-T ORR was 82% for bridging responders, 71% for stable disease, and 52% for progressive disease (p<0.001). Median PFS was 18.2, 9.4, and 3.8 months respectively. Patients with progressive disease during bridging had 2.6-fold higher grade 3+ CRS rates (22% vs 8.5%, p=0.003), likely reflecting higher inflammatory tumor burden at infusion. Bridging regimen type (chemo vs radiation vs targeted) was less predictive than disease response status. These data suggest that disease kinetics during the bridging interval, rather than bridging therapy itself, drive post-CAR-T outcomes.

Real-world registry analysis of 195 triple-class refractory multiple myeloma patients receiving ciltacabtagene autoleucel (Carvykti) at 16 US centers (2022-2024). Overall response rate was 73% with 42% sCR/CR and 18% VGPR. Median PFS was 16.4 months. Grade 3+ CRS occurred in 4% and grade 3+ ICANS in 7%. Movement and neurocognitive toxicity (MNT) of any grade was observed in 6.7%, with grade 3+ MNT in 1.5%, lower than the CARTITUDE-1 trial (grade 3+ MNT 5%). Median time from leukapheresis to infusion was 42 days. Patients with 3-4 prior lines of therapy (n=82) had superior outcomes compared to those with 5+ prior lines (n=113): ORR 82% vs 67% (p=0.02), consistent with earlier use benefiting patients. MRD negativity at 10^-5 was achieved in 58% of evaluable responders.

Multi-center registry analysis of 156 patients receiving a second CAR-T infusion after relapse following initial CAR-T therapy for LBCL (n=98) or B-ALL (n=58) across 22 US and European centers (2019-2024). Overall ORR to second infusion was 42% (LBCL 38%, B-ALL 48%). Among those receiving a different CAR-T product (n=87), ORR was 52% compared to 29% for same-product re-infusion (n=69, p=0.004). Median duration of response from second infusion was 6.2 months. Grade 3+ CRS was lower on retreatment (6%) compared to first infusion (14%), possibly reflecting reduced tumor burden. Anti-CAR antibodies (HAMA/HAHA) were detected in 28% of re-treated patients and correlated with poor second-infusion expansion (p=0.001). Switching from murine (FMC63) to humanized scFv-based CAR for second infusion improved outcomes when HAMA was present (ORR 61% vs 18%). Median OS from second infusion was 11.4 months.
outcome_value: 42% overall; 52% different product vs 29% same product

text_summary: CIBMTR registry analysis of 4,328 CAR-T recipients (2018-2024): Largest real-world dataset for CAR-T outcomes. DLBCL patients (n=2,841): ORR 62%, CR 42%, median PFS 7.8 months, median OS 18.4 months. Myeloma patients (n=1,487): ORR 74%, CR 42%, median PFS 11.2 months. Outcomes modestly inferior to clinical trials (trial ORR 72-83%) but clinically meaningful in real-world population including trial-ineligible patients. Predictors of inferior outcome: ECOG ≥2 (HR 2.1), LDH >2x ULN (HR 1.8), ≥4 prior lines (HR 1.5), bulky disease >10cm (HR 1.4). Non-relapse mortality 4.2% at 1 year. Data supports CAR-T as standard of care across both academic and community settings.
outcome_value: DLBCL ORR 62%, CR 42%; MM ORR 74%, CR 42%

text_summary: Community vs academic center outcomes comparison: Multicenter analysis of 1,892 patients at 45 sites (23 academic, 22 community). Academic centers (n=1,247): ORR 68%, CR 47%, 1yr PFS 38%, CRS grade ≥3 6%, ICANS grade ≥3 12%. Community certified centers (n=645): ORR 63%, CR 41%, 1yr PFS 34%, CRS grade ≥3 5%, ICANS grade ≥3 9%. Adjusted difference in ORR: 3.2% favoring academic (95% CI: -0.8 to 7.2%, p=0.12 — not significant). Key finding: community centers with FACT-accredited cell therapy programs achieve comparable outcomes. Disparities exist in referral patterns: median time from CAR-T eligibility to referral was 21 days longer for community patients, and 23% of eligible community patients were never referred.

Elderly patients (≥75 years) real-world CAR-T outcomes: 284 patients ≥75 years (median 78, range 75-89) at 22 academic centers. Products: axi-cel 50%, liso-cel 31%, tisa-cel 11%, ide-cel 8%. ORR 62%, CR 43%. Median PFS 7.2 months, median OS 14.8 months. CRS grade ≥3: 8% (vs 5% younger). ICANS grade ≥3: 15% (vs 10%). ICU admission: 28%. NRM at 1 year: 8% (vs 3%). Subgroup analysis: ECOG 0-1 patients (n=198): ORR 71%, PFS 11.3 months — comparable to younger. ECOG ≥2 (n=86): ORR 42%, PFS 3.1 months. Conclusion: functional status is the primary determinant, not chronological age.

Racial and ethnic disparities in CAR-T access and outcomes: Population-based analysis using linked SEER-Medicare + commercial claims (2021-2024). 8,247 clinically eligible patients. CAR-T utilization: White 15.8%, Black 8.2%, Hispanic 7.1%, Asian 9.4% (p<0.001). Adjusted OR for Black patients receiving CAR-T: 0.58 (95% CI 0.49-0.69). Barriers: distance to center (median 78 miles rural vs 12 miles urban), caregiver requirement, insurance authorization delays (Medicaid 21 days vs commercial 8 days), implicit referral bias. Among those receiving CAR-T, outcomes were similar: White ORR 71%, Black 69%, Hispanic 72% (p=NS). The disparity is in access, not biology. Recommendations: satellite apheresis centers, tele-oncology triage, navigator programs, standardized referral criteria.
outcome_value: White 15.8%, Black 8.2%, Hispanic 7.1%. Outcomes equivalent when accessed

text_summary: Insurance and financial toxicity analysis: Survey of 412 CAR-T recipients + claims data. Total cost of care (from apheresis through 1-year follow-up): median $528,000 (IQR $387K-$714K). Breakdown: CAR-T product 65%, inpatient hospitalization 18%, post-discharge care 12%, apheresis/conditioning 5%. Out-of-pocket costs: commercial insurance median $12,400; Medicare median $8,200; Medicaid minimal. Financial distress (COST score <26): 38% of commercially insured, 28% of Medicare. Outcomes-based contracts (e.g., Novartis Kymriah): 20% of payers adopted, reduced payer risk but increased administrative burden. Value-based pricing models: $300K-500K QALY-adjusted threshold generally met for 2L DLBCL (ICER ~$120K/QALY) but borderline for 3L+ myeloma (ICER ~$280K/QALY).
primary_endpoint: Total cost of care, financial distress score
outcome_value: Median total cost $528K, financial distress in 38% commercially insured

text_summary: Geographic access disparities: GIS analysis of 4,891 eligible patients. Only 38% of US population lives within 50 miles of a CAR-T certified center. Patients >100 miles from center: 55% lower likelihood of receiving CAR-T (adjusted OR 0.45). Rural patients: 3.2x longer from diagnosis to CAR-T infusion (median 97 vs 30 days). Mortality during wait: rural 11% vs urban 4%. Proposed solutions: regional apheresis collection sites (reduces travel to 1 trip vs 5-7), point-of-care manufacturing, tele-oncology consultation for eligibility screening. States with lowest access: Wyoming, Montana, North Dakota, Mississippi, West Virginia (<10% population within 50 miles of center).
primary_endpoint: CAR-T utilization by distance to center
outcome_value: >100 miles: OR 0.45 for CAR-T receipt; rural mortality during wait 11%

text_summary: Time-to-infusion real-world metrics: Analysis of 2,156 commercial CAR-T infusions. Vein-to-vein time: axi-cel median 29 days (IQR 24-38), liso-cel median 37 days (30-48), tisa-cel median 40 days (32-55), ide-cel median 47 days (38-60), cilta-cel median 77 days (60-95). Patient attrition from leukapheresis to infusion: 12% overall (death 5%, progression 4%, manufacturing failure 3%). Attrition highest for cilta-cel (18%) due to longer manufacturing. Bridging therapy administered in 68% of DLBCL patients. Predictors of long vein-to-vein: manufacturing slot availability, insurance authorization delays, conditioning scheduling. Same-site apheresis and manufacturing (when available) reduced vein-to-vein by median 8 days.

Hospital readmission rates post CAR-T: Analysis of 1,834 patients discharged after CAR-T. 30-day readmission rate: 32%. 90-day readmission rate: 48%. Most common readmission reasons: infection (28%), cytopenias requiring transfusion (22%), delayed CRS/ICANS (15%), disease progression (12%), other (23%). Median readmission length of stay: 5 days (IQR 3-9). ICU readmission: 8% of all readmissions. Readmission risk factors: grade ≥2 CRS during initial stay (OR 2.1), discharge before day 14 (OR 1.8), hypogammaglobulinemia at discharge (OR 1.6). Outpatient CAR-T feasibility: 142 patients managed entirely outpatient — 30-day readmission 28% (similar to inpatient discharged). No mortality difference. Suggests outpatient management is feasible for select low-risk patients.

Total cost of care breakdown: Comprehensive cost analysis from payer perspective, 892 patients. Pre-treatment: $28,000 (workup, imaging, apheresis, bridging). CAR-T product: $373,000 (list price). Inpatient: $85,000 (median 12 days, ICU 28%). Post-discharge 0-30 days: $32,000 (readmissions, ER visits, transfusions). Post-discharge 31-365 days: $47,000 (monitoring, IVIG, infections, imaging). Total year 1: $528,000 median. Responders (CR at 6 months): total cost $495,000; QALYs gained 4.2; ICER $118K/QALY. Non-responders: total cost $562,000; QALYs gained 0.8; ICER $703K/QALY. Strategies to reduce cost: POC manufacturing ($30-50K savings), outpatient administration ($20-40K savings), shortened monitoring ($10-15K savings).
primary_endpoint: Total cost of care, ICER analysis
outcome_value: Total year 1: $528K. Responder ICER $118K/QALY. Cost reduction opportunities $60-105K

text_summary: CAR-T in frail/comorbid patients (HCT-CI ≥3): 186 patients with high comorbidity index (HCT-CI ≥3) receiving commercial CAR-T. Compared to low comorbidity (HCT-CI 0-2, n=1,024): ORR 58% vs 69% (p=0.008), NRM 11% vs 3% (p<0.001), ICU admission 35% vs 17% (p<0.001). Major comorbidities: cardiac (EF <50%) — higher tocilizumab use, CRS-associated troponin elevation 42%; renal (eGFR <45) — higher CRS severity, longer hospital stay; hepatic — higher transaminitis. Despite higher toxicity, median OS of 11.2 months provides meaningful benefit for patients with no other options. Modified lymphodepletion (reduced Flu/Cy) in CKD patients had similar CAR-T expansion with less renal toxicity.
primary_endpoint: ORR, NRM, ICU admission in comorbid patients
outcome_value: ORR 58%, NRM 11%, ICU 35%. Meaningful OS 11.2 months despite comorbidities

text_summary: Prior lines of therapy impact: 2L vs 3L+ outcomes: CIBMTR analysis of 2,841 DLBCL patients. 2L (n=1,234): ORR 72%, CR 52%, median PFS 14.2 months, median OS 28.4 months. 3L (n=987): ORR 65%, CR 43%, median PFS 8.1 months, OS 19.2 months. ≥4L (n=620): ORR 55%, CR 35%, median PFS 5.4 months, OS 12.8 months. CRS/ICANS rates similar across lines. Key difference: T-cell fitness — apheresis products from 2L patients had 2.3x higher naive T-cell frequency, 1.8x higher mitochondrial mass, and 40% lower exhaustion marker expression. Earlier CAR-T produces better products and better outcomes. Health economic analysis: 2L CAR-T is most cost-effective (ICER $95K/QALY vs $180K/QALY for ≥4L).
primary_endpoint: ORR and PFS by line of therapy
outcome_value: 2L ORR 72%, PFS 14.2mo; ≥4L ORR 55%, PFS 5.4mo. Earlier = better

text_summary: International variations in CAR-T access (2025): Global analysis of CAR-T availability. USA: 6 approved products, ~5,000 infusions/year, 180+ certified centers. EU: 4 approved products, ~2,500 infusions/year, 120+ centers. China: extensive off-label and clinical trial use, estimated 3,000+ infusions/year, but no commercially approved product through standard pathway (3 NMPA approvals pending). Japan: 2 approved products (PMDA), ~300 infusions/year, 25 centers. Israel: 3 approved products, ~200 infusions/year, all at 4 academic centers. India: no approved products, clinical trials only, estimated 50 infusions/year. Sub-Saharan Africa: <10 patients treated (clinical trials only). Global inequality: 95% of CAR-T infusions occur in 5 countries representing 15% of global cancer burden.
primary_endpoint: CAR-T infusions per year by country
outcome_value: USA 5000, EU 2500, China 3000+, Japan 300, rest minimal. 95% in 5 countries

text_summary: Outpatient CAR-T administration feasibility: Prospective study of 142 patients receiving CAR-T entirely outpatient at 8 centers (daily clinic visits days 0-14, 24/7 tele-monitoring). Eligibility: ECOG 0-1, live within 30 min of center, reliable caregiver, no prior severe infections. Products: liso-cel (n=78), tisa-cel (n=38), axi-cel (n=26). ORR 67% (comparable to inpatient). CRS any grade: 72% (treated outpatient with subcutaneous tocilizumab in 8%). Hospitalization required: 32% (CRS 18%, ICANS 8%, infection 6%). ICU transfer: 5%. No treatment-related deaths. Patient satisfaction: 91% preferred outpatient. Cost savings: $42,000 per patient average (eliminated 10-14 inpatient days). Conclusion: outpatient CAR-T feasible for select patients with liso-cel/tisa-cel; axi-cel higher hospitalization rate (46%).
outcome_value: Outpatient successful in 68%; cost savings $42K; satisfaction 91%

text_summary: Long-term survivor quality of life (≥2 years post CAR-T): Survey of 324 patients alive and disease-free ≥2 years post CAR-T. FACT-Lym scores: 78% reported good-to-excellent overall QoL. Persistent issues: fatigue (48%), cognitive difficulties (22%), anxiety about relapse (65%), financial stress (38%). Return to work: 62% (of those employed pre-CAR-T). IVIG dependence: 18% at 2 years. Recurrent infections: 24% had ≥2 infections requiring antibiotics in year 2. Functional status: 72% ECOG 0, 21% ECOG 1, 7% ECOG ≥2. Comparison to matched non-CAR-T cancer survivors: CAR-T survivors reported higher fatigue and infection rates but similar overall QoL and lower cancer-related anxiety (due to deeper responses). Unmet needs: long-term survivorship clinics, financial counseling, cognitive rehabilitation.
primary_endpoint: FACT-Lym QoL score, return to work, persistent symptoms
outcome_value: 78% good-excellent QoL; 62% return to work; fatigue 48%; IVIG 18%

text_summary: Caregiver burden in CAR-T therapy: Prospective survey of 186 patient-caregiver dyads. Caregiver Burden Inventory: moderate-severe burden in 52% during acute phase (days 0-30), declining to 28% at 6 months. Mean daily caregiving hours: 14h (days 0-14), 8h (days 14-30), 4h (months 1-3), 2h (months 3-6). Caregiver employment impact: 45% reduced work hours, 22% stopped working temporarily, 8% quit job permanently. Caregiver distress (PHQ-9 depression screening): moderate-severe in 31% at 1 month, 18% at 6 months. Top stressors: uncertainty (78%), medication management (62%), emergency recognition (58%), transportation (45%), financial strain (42%). Support needs: 82% wanted more education about what to expect, 68% wanted peer support groups, 55% wanted respite care. Caregiver burden correlated with patient CRS severity (r=0.48) and hospital readmissions (r=0.41).
outcome_value: Moderate-severe burden 52% acute, 28% at 6mo; 45% reduced work

text_summary: CAR-T utilization trends 2020-2025: National analysis of commercial CAR-T utilization. Annual infusions in US: 2020: 1,200; 2021: 2,100; 2022: 3,400; 2023: 4,200; 2024: 5,100; 2025 (projected): 6,200. CAGR: 38%. Growth drivers: new indications (2L DLBCL added 2022, FL/MZL 2024, earlier-line MM 2024), new products (cilta-cel 2022, liso-cel FL 2024), expanded center network (120 → 185 certified centers). Unmet demand: estimated 15,000-20,000 eligible patients/year vs 6,200 treated (utilization gap: 60-70%). Barriers to closing gap: manufacturing capacity, center certification timeline (12-18 months), referral patterns, payer authorization. Projection: 10,000 annual infusions by 2027 if manufacturing capacity scales and access programs expand.
